column2: Invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) in a whole slide image typically presents as a diffuse, infiltrative pattern with irregular, single-file or scattered clusters of neoplastic cells that lack the classic architectural features seen in other breast carcinomas like ductal or lobular structures. The tumor cells often have a monomorphic appearance with scant cytoplasm and can show mild to moderate pleomorphism. These cells frequently invade along the interstitium and can be seen pushing the normal breast stroma without true formation of ducts or lobules. ILC may also display a more infiltrative and less organized growth pattern compared to invasive ductal carcinoma, sometimes resembling a "crazy paving" or "foggy" appearance due to the diffuse involvement and the presence of inflammatory cells. The background may show areas of fibrosis and lymphocytic infiltration, which can help distinguish it from other types of breast cancer.

Invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) in a whole slide image typically presents as a diffuse, infiltrative pattern with irregular, single-file or scattered clusters of neoplastic cells that lack the classic architectural features seen in other breast carcinomas like ductal or lobular structures. The tumor cells often have a monomorphic appearance with scant cytoplasm and can show mild to moderate pleomorphism. These cells frequently infiltrate along the interstitium, particularly around blood vessels and lymphatics, and may exhibit a "single file" or "streaming" pattern of invasion. The background stroma is often edematous, and there can be an associated inflammatory response with lymphocytic infiltration. Nodularity and microcalcifications, which are more characteristic of ductal carcinomas, are uncommon in ILC. The tumor cells may also express estrogen receptor (ER), progesterone receptor (PR), and HER2, but HER2 overexpression is less common compared to other breast carcinomas.

Invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) in a whole slide image typically presents as a diffuse, infiltrative pattern with irregularly shaped, single or clustered, lobular or acinar-like structures that lack the classic architectural features seen in ductal carcinomas. The tumor cells often have a monomorphic appearance with small, round to oval nuclei and scant cytoplasm, and they may exhibit mild pleomorphism. ILC tends to invade along the interstitium without forming distinct ducts or acini, which can make it challenging to distinguish from reactive or inflammatory changes. The stroma surrounding these areas is often edematous and can be infiltrated by lymphocytes, forming a lymphocytic cuff around the tumor nests. Hemorrhage and necrosis are less common compared to other breast carcinomas but may be observed. The background may show fibrous tissue proliferation and occasional fibrovascular proliferation, contributing to a more diffuse and less organized growth pattern compared to invasive ductal carcinoma.

Invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) in a whole slide image typically presents as a diffuse, infiltrative pattern with irregular, single-file or scattered clusters of neoplastic cells that lack the classic architectural features seen in ductal carcinomas. The tumor cells often have a monomorphic appearance with scant cytoplasm and can show mild to moderate pleomorphism. These cells frequently invade along the interlobular septa and can extend into the stroma in a single file or in small groups, which may appear as a "single file of soldiers" or "streaming" pattern. ILC often lacks the formation of true ducts or acini, and when present, these structures are usually distorted and irregular. Additionally, ILC is commonly associated with lymphocytic infiltration and can have a more inflammatory background compared to other breast carcinomas. The stroma surrounding the tumor cells is often edematous and can be hypercellular, contributing to the overall diffuse and infiltrative growth pattern observed in ILC.

Invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) in a whole slide image typically presents as a diffuse, infiltrative pattern with irregular, single-file or scattered clusters of neoplastic cells that lack the classic architectural features seen in ductal carcinomas. The tumor cells often have a monomorphic appearance with scant cytoplasm and can show mild to moderate pleomorphism. These cells frequently invade along the interlobular septa and can extend into the stroma without forming distinct glandular structures. Nodularity and microcalcifications are less common compared to ductal carcinomas. The background stroma may appear edematous or fibrotic, and there is often an inflammatory response present, characterized by a mixed population of lymphocytes, plasma cells, and macrophages. Capillary-like vascular spaces may be observed within the tumor, and the tumor cells may exhibit varying degrees of nuclear atypia and mitotic activity.

Invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) in a whole slide image typically presents as a diffuse, infiltrative pattern with irregular, single-file or scattered clusters of neoplastic cells that lack the classic architectural features seen in ductal carcinomas. The tumor cells often have a monomorphic appearance with scant cytoplasm and can show mild to moderate pleomorphism. ILC may present with a background of lymphocytic infiltration and fibrosis, which can be more prominent compared to other breast carcinomas. Capillary-like vascular spaces may be observed within the tumor, and the stroma surrounding the tumor cells can appear edematous. Overall, the invasive nature of ILC is characterized by its diffuse, infiltrative growth pattern and the absence of the typical acinar or papillary architecture seen in other breast carcinomas.

Invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) in a whole slide image typically presents as a diffuse, infiltrative pattern with irregular, single-file or scattered clusters of neoplastic cells that lack the classic architectural features seen in other breast carcinomas like ductal or lobular structures. The tumor cells often have a monomorphic appearance with scant cytoplasm and can show mild to moderate pleomorphism. They frequently invade along the basement membrane and between normal breast stroma, giving a "foggy" or "ground-glass" appearance when viewed under the microscope. ILC may also present with areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, and fibrosis, which can be seen in the image as areas of disorganization and disruption of the normal tissue architecture. The background stroma is often edematous and can contain lymphoplasmacytic infiltration, which may appear as a dense, inflammatory response surrounding the tumor.

Invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) in a whole slide image typically presents as a diffuse, infiltrative pattern with irregular, single-file or scattered clusters of neoplastic cells that lack the classic architectural features seen in other breast carcinomas like ductal or lobular structures. The tumor cells often have a monomorphic appearance with small, round to polygonal nuclei and scant cytoplasm. They may infiltrate the stroma in a single file or in a "single file" or "streaming" pattern, which is characteristic of ILC. The background stroma is often edematous and can contain lymphoplasmacytic infiltration, which is more pronounced compared to other types of breast cancer. Additionally, ILC may show areas of necrosis and hemorrhage, and the tumor cells may exhibit pleomorphism and anaplasia, though these features are less common than in other invasive breast carcinomas. The overall architecture lacks the typical acinar or cribriform patterns seen in invasive ductal carcinoma, making it distinct in its visual presentation.

Invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC) in a whole slide image typically presents as a diffuse, infiltrative pattern with irregularly shaped, single or clustered, small, round to polygonal cells that often have a bland appearance and lack significant atypia. These neoplastic cells infiltrate the stroma in a single file or in small clusters, without the formation of true glandular structures. The background is often edematous with a lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate, and there may be areas of hemorrhage and necrosis. The tumor cells may show mild pleomorphism and have indistinct borders, with occasional mitotic figures present but not in high frequency. The stroma surrounding the tumor cells is often rich and can appear as a "starry sky" appearance due to the presence of numerous small vessels and lymphocytes. Capillary-like vascular channels are commonly observed, and the tumor may extend into the breast stroma in a lobular or nodular pattern, which helps distinguish it from invasive ductal carcinoma.
